responses: We built an AI system for automated legal contract drafting and negotiation position generation at a mid-market commercial law firm. The system generates first-draft contract language from a structured deal template, identifies non-standard clauses in counterparty paper, generates redlines with alternative formulations ranked by aggressiveness, and produces a negotiation position memo that characterizes each open issue, the client's priority, the likely counterparty objection, and the acceptable resolution range. The system is used by junior associates to prepare negotiation packages for partner review. The problem it solves is that our junior associates were spending 60-70% of their billable time on first-draft work that partners spent significant time revising. Post-deployment over 20 months: partner revision time on associate first drafts has dropped by 55%, associate time to produce a negotiation-ready package has dropped from 12 hours to 4 hours, and the firm's capacity to handle mid-market commercial transactions has increased by 38% without adding associate headcount. We measure output quality through quarterly partner evaluations of AI-assisted versus non-AI-assisted work products on a blind basis; AI-assisted packages score at or above non-AI-assisted packages on 7 of 8 evaluation dimensions, with the exception of novel deal structure recognition where associates still outperform the AI-assisted workflow.

This system's architecture was designed around a constraint that drove every other decision: the outputs must be attributable to and defensible by a licensed attorney. This ruled out any approach where the system could produce legal conclusions autonomously — everything the system generates must be reviewable and endorseable by the attorney before it becomes client advice. Given that constraint, we evaluated three approaches: a template library with automated fill-in, where the system populates a library of pre-approved clause variants; a retrieval-augmented drafting system using the firm's historical contract library as the retrieval corpus; and an LLM drafting system with a structured review layer. We rejected the template library because commercial contracts have too much variation at the clause interaction level — a standard IP assignment clause from the library becomes non-standard when combined with certain payment terms and international jurisdiction clauses in ways that the template library could not anticipate. The RAG approach using our historical contract library was appealing because it grounded outputs in the firm's established practice, but it introduced a selection bias risk — the retrieval corpus encoded the negotiation outcomes we had accepted, not the positions we had sought and failed to get, which would bias the system toward positions that underperformed our clients' interests. We chose the LLM drafting system because it could generate novel formulations without being constrained by historical outcomes, but we added two structural constraints: all AI-generated clause language is displayed with a confidence rating derived from similarity to the firm's validated clause library, and any clause not in the top 80th percentile of confidence requires an explicit associate review note before it can appear in a draft submitted for partner review.

Our risk register has 10 items. The five most consequential: First, the system generates a clause that is unenforceable under governing law, and this passes associate and partner review, resulting in a contract that cannot be enforced as written. Mitigation: all AI-generated clauses are checked against a jurisdiction-specific enforceability database maintained by the firm's litigation group; clauses that fail the check are blocked from output, not just flagged. The enforceability database is updated quarterly from new case law and statutory changes. Monitoring: we track the enforceability check rejection rate by clause type; a sustained increase in rejections for a clause type triggers a review of the LLM's clause generation patterns for that type. Recovery: if an unenforceable clause is discovered post-execution, we have a defined process for assessing re-negotiation options and notification obligations. Second, the system's negotiation position memo mischaracterizes the client's acceptable resolution range due to a misparse of the deal template or client instructions. Mitigation: negotiation position memos require explicit client confirmation of the stated acceptable resolution ranges before the associate can incorporate them in a negotiation package. Third, a previous client's confidential contract language enters the training corpus or retrieval pool and is surfaced in work for a different client, creating a confidentiality breach. Mitigation: the retrieval corpus is partitioned by client matter; cross-client retrieval is architecturally disabled, not just policy-prohibited. We test this partition quarterly using synthetic retrieval probes. Fourth, the system develops a systematic bias toward clause formulations that favor one party type — buyer vs seller, or licensor vs licensee — due to imbalance in the training or retrieval corpus. Mitigation: we run a balance analysis semi-annually, comparing AI-generated positions on matched deal types with different party perspectives. Fifth, an associate uses the AI-generated confidence rating as a substitute for legal judgment — treating high-confidence clauses as pre-approved when they still require independent analysis. Mitigation: high-confidence clauses include a visible reminder that confidence reflects similarity to historical practice, not legal validation; associates are required to complete a training module on AI-assisted review before system access is granted.

Working in legal AI has produced my clearest thinking about the conditions under which AI output should and should not be trusted, because the consequences of getting it wrong are concrete and attributable. I use a three-factor test before delegating any decision category to AI in this context: Can I independently verify the output without reconstructing the underlying reasoning? Does the error rate in this output category, under the conditions of use, produce an expected harm that is acceptable given the available review process? And would I be comfortable explaining this delegation decision to a client, a partner, or a regulator? I developed this framework through specific failures. In month 6, the system produced a limitation of liability clause that was correctly formulated under the model contract but did not account for a carve-out that the client had negotiated in a prior contract with the same counterparty — information that was in the deal template but that the system had not integrated. The associate, relying on the high confidence rating, submitted the draft without catching the inconsistency. The partner caught it in review. After that incident, I mapped every decision category in the system against the three-factor test and found that negotiation position generation failed the first factor in 30% of cases — the associate could not independently verify the position without substantial legal analysis. We restructured that output category to produce reasoning chains, not just positions, so that associates had the material to verify. The second major incident: the system confidently generated alternative language for a force majeure clause using formulations from cases decided before 2020. The associate recognized this was a fast-evolving area of law and flagged it rather than accepting the AI's output. We added a vintage filter that flags any clause formulation pattern not found in case law or contracts from the last 24 months, and converted that dimension of the system from AI-generated to associate-drafted with AI-suggested precedents. These two incidents together produced the clearest illustration I have encountered of the difference between AI competence (generating plausible legal language) and AI judgment (knowing when that language is right for this situation).

rationale: L5 with two 4s. Boundary earns 4 — the scope constraints are described but not comprehensively enumerated across all excluded problem types. Blast radius earns 4 — consequences of each failure mode are described with containment, but progressive rollback procedures are not present for all cases. Override evidence earns 5 — a proactive three-factor framework with clear criteria, not just reactive incident response, and examples of associates applying the framework correctly to catch AI errors.
